pattern_recognition: This problem falls under the category of 'Array Manipulation' and 'Floyd's Cycle Detection'. The presence of duplicate numbers in a constrained range signals that we can leverage properties of cycles in a linked list representation of the array.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check each element against all others to find a duplicate.
approaches.algorithm: Iterate through the array with two nested loops, comparing each element with every other element.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Utilize the fact that the array elements can be treated as pointers to indices, allowing us to detect cycles.
approaches.algorithm: Use Floyd's Tortoise and Hare algorithm to find the intersection point in the cycle formed by the duplicate.

dry_run: Consider the input array [3, 1, 3, 4, 2]. Start with two pointers, slow and fast. Initially, both point to the first element. Move slow by one step and fast by two steps until they meet. Once they meet, reset one pointer to the start and move both one step at a time until they meet again, which will be the duplicate number.
edge_cases: An empty input array should return an error or null. A single element array should also return an error since no duplicates can exist. If the array contains all unique elements except one duplicate, it should correctly identify that duplicate. Large inputs should be handled efficiently without exceeding memory limits.
common_pitfalls: 1. Not handling the case of an empty array or single element correctly. 2. Assuming that the input will always contain a duplicate, leading to incorrect assumptions. 3. Failing to implement the optimal approach correctly, resulting in higher time complexity than necessary.
